title: L1: GCSE Loops
description: GCSE-style loop questions: total, count, highest/lowest, while loops with sentinels and validation. Endless mode with real exam-style problems.

steps.title: GCSE Style Questions
steps.content:
Real GCSE Loop Questions 📚

This lesson covers the kind of loop questions you'll meet in a GCSE exam. They look hard, but they all use the same handful of patterns over and over!

You'll learn:

  • How to convert input text into numbers using int() and float()
  • How to make smart prompt messages using + and str()
  • The total pattern (adding things up)
  • The count pattern (counting how many)
  • The highest / lowest patterns
  • How to use while loops with stop values
  • How to validate input with or

💡 Each pattern has a small "have a go" example, then practice questions, ending with random GCSE-style questions in Endless Mode.

Ready? Click Next Step.

steps.title: int(input(...))
steps.content:
Turning words into numbers

When you ask the user a question with input, the answer always comes back as text — even if they type a number.

Watch what happens if you try to add 10 to a text answer:

age = input("How old are you? ")
print(age + 10)   # ERROR! Can't add a number to text

To fix this, wrap the input in int(...). This converts text into a whole number:

age = int(input("How old are you? "))
print("Next year you will be", age + 1)

⚠️ Watch the brackets carefully! There are two closing brackets at the end:

int(input("How old are you? "))

One closes input( and one closes int(.

Have a go! ✏️

Click Run, type any whole number, and press Enter.
steps.js: PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: "editor-int", initialCode: "age = int(input(\"How old are you? \"))\nprint(\"Next year you will be\", age + 1)", label: "int(input)" });

steps.title: float(input(...))
steps.content:
For numbers with decimals

int only works for whole numbers. If you need numbers with decimals — like prices, weights, or temperatures — use float instead.

price = float(input("Enter the price: "))
print("With VAT:", price * 1.2)
Use thisFor
int(input(...))Whole numbers (5, 100, -3)
float(input(...))Decimal numbers (5.5, 3.14, 99.99)

Have a go! ✏️

Click Run, type a decimal price like 9.99.
steps.js: PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: "editor-float", initialCode: "price = float(input(\"Enter the price: \"))\nprint(\"With VAT:\", price * 1.2)", label: "float(input)" });

steps.title: Practice: Numbers
steps.content:
Mini Practice 🎯

Ask the user for their height in metres (a decimal number) and then double it. Show:

Double your height: 3.4

💡 The validator will type 1.7. Use float(input(...)).

steps.title: Building Prompts
steps.content:
Building messages with +

Sometimes you need a prompt that changes each time round a loop, like asking for "items sold for day 1", "items sold for day 2", and so on.

You can join pieces of text together with +:

name = "Sam"
greeting = "Hello, " + name + "!"
print(greeting)

Output:

Hello, Sam!

⚠️ You can only use + to join text with text. To put a number into text, wrap the number with str(...):

day = 3
print("Day " + str(day))   # works!
print("Day " + day)        # ERROR! Number not text

This is super useful for prompts in a loop. Try this:
steps.js: PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: "editor-strcat", initialCode: "for day in range(1, 4):\n    name = input(\"Enter name for day \" + str(day) + \": \")\n    print(\"Day \" + str(day) + \":\", name)", label: "Building prompts" });

steps.title: The Total Pattern
steps.content:
Pattern 1: Adding things up

The first big pattern! Lots of GCSE questions ask you to add a list of numbers up. Every time, you do the same three things:

Step 1. Make a variable called total and set it to 0

Step 2. Loop, asking for each number and doing total = total + number

Step 3. After the loop, print the total

Here's an example that adds up 5 numbers:

total = 0

for i in range(1, 6):
    number = int(input("Enter number " + str(i) + ": "))
    total = total + number

print("Total:", total)

💡 Notice: we use range(1, 6) not range(5). We want the loop variable to count 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 so the prompt says "Enter number 1" not "Enter number 0".

Have a go! ✏️

Run it and type any 5 numbers!

steps.title: Practice: Total
steps.content:
Mini Practice 🎯

Ask the user for the price of 3 items, then print the total cost.

The validator will type 5.00, 10.00, 3.50. Your output should contain 18.5.

Total cost: 18.5

💡 Use float(input(...)) for prices and follow the total pattern!

steps.title: The Count Pattern
steps.content:
Pattern 2: Counting how many

This pattern looks almost exactly like the total pattern, but there's one big difference:

The total pattern adds the value to the total: total = total + number

The count pattern adds 1 to the count: count = count + 1

Counting only happens when something is true — so we use it with an if statement. Here's an example that counts how many of 5 marks are passes (50 or higher):

passes = 0

for i in range(1, 6):
    mark = int(input("Enter mark " + str(i) + ": "))
    if mark >= 50:
        passes = passes + 1

print("Passes:", passes)

The three steps are:

  1. Set passes to 0 before the loop
  2. In the loop, check if the condition is met (mark >= 50)
  3. If yes, do passes = passes + 1

Have a go! ✏️

Run it. Try entering some marks above and below 50.

steps.title: Practice: Count
steps.content:
Mini Practice 🎯

Loop 5 times, asking for a number each time. Count how many numbers are more than 10. Print the count.

The validator types 5, 12, 3, 20, 15 (three of those are over 10).

💡 Use if number > 10:

steps.title: The Highest Pattern
steps.content:
Pattern 3: Finding the highest

Imagine you record temperatures for 7 days and want to know the warmest. You could just look at the list — but a computer needs a method!

The trick: keep a variable called highest. Each time round the loop, if the new number is bigger than what's in highest, replace it.

highest = -999

for day in range(1, 8):
    temp = int(input("Day " + str(day) + " temperature: "))
    if temp > highest:
        highest = temp

print("Highest temperature:", highest)

⚠️ Why start with -999? We want the first temperature to definitely beat highest so it gets stored. Any real temperature will be bigger than -999.

For finding the lowest, do the opposite: start with a huge number like 9999 and use < instead of >:

lowest = 9999

for day in range(1, 8):
    temp = int(input("Day " + str(day) + ": "))
    if temp < lowest:
        lowest = temp

print("Lowest:", lowest)

Have a go! ✏️

Run the highest example. Try a few different numbers!

steps.title: Practice: Highest
steps.content:
Mini Practice 🎯

Ask for 4 scores and print the lowest one.

The validator types 50, 30, 40, 20. The lowest is 20.

💡 Start with lowest = 9999 and check if score < lowest:

steps.title: while: Stop on a value
steps.content:
While loops with a stop value

Sometimes you don't know how many numbers the user will type. They keep going until they enter a special "stop" value, like -1.

This special value is called a sentinel. To handle it, use this exact pattern:

total = 0

# Step 1: Ask the FIRST time before the loop
number = int(input("Enter a number (-1 to finish): "))

# Step 2: Loop while we haven't seen -1
while number != -1:
    total = total + number
    # Step 3: Ask AGAIN at the bottom of the loop
    number = int(input("Enter a number (-1 to finish): "))

print("Total:", total)

💡 Two input lines! One before the loop (to get started) and one at the bottom of the loop (to ask again). This is called the "prime the pump" pattern.

Trace through it carefully:

  1. Ask once. User types 10.
  2. 10 != -1? Yes → total = 10. Ask again. User types 20.
  3. 20 != -1? Yes → total = 30. Ask again. User types -1.
  4. -1 != -1? No! Stop. Print total: 30.

Have a go! ✏️

Run it. Type any numbers, then -1 to stop.

steps.title: Practice: while sum
steps.content:
Mini Practice 🎯

Use a while loop to count how many numbers the user enters until they enter 0. (Don't count the 0 itself.) Print the count.

The validator types 5, 8, 12, 3, 0. The answer is 4.

💡 Remember the prime-the-pump pattern — one input before the loop, and another inside.

steps.title: while with or
steps.content:
Validating with or

Sometimes you keep asking until the answer is in a valid range. The condition has two parts joined with or:

age = int(input("Enter age (must be 11-18): "))

while age < 11 or age > 18:
    print("That's not in range!")
    age = int(input("Enter age (must be 11-18): "))

print("Valid age:", age)

Read the while line out loud:

💬 "Keep going while age is less than 11 or age is greater than 18."

If either is true, the number is invalid → loop again.

Only when both are false (so age is between 11 and 18) does the loop stop.

Same prime-the-pump shape: one input before, one inside.

Have a go! ✏️

Try entering bad ages first (like 5 or 25) and then a valid one (like 14).

steps.title: GCSE: Items Sold
steps.content:
GCSE Question 1 📝

Write a Python program that calculates the total number of items sold in a week.

Your program should:

  • prompt the user to enter the number of items sold each day
  • repeat this input process for 7 days
  • calculate the total number of items sold
  • output the total in an appropriate message

💡 This is the total pattern you just learned! The validator will type 10, 20, 15, 5, 10, 20, 20. The total is 100.

steps.title: GCSE: Highest Temp
steps.content:
GCSE Question 2 📝

Write a Python program that determines the highest temperature recorded in a week.

Your program should:

  • prompt the user to enter the temperature for each day
  • repeat this input process for 7 days
  • determine which temperature is the highest
  • output the highest temperature in an appropriate message

💡 The highest pattern! Validator types 10, 25, 15, 32, 20, 5, 12. Answer: 32.

steps.title: Endless Mode
steps.content:
Endless GCSE Practice ∞

Real GCSE-style questions, randomly chosen each round. Some need for loops, some need while loops!

Streak: 0
Best: 0
steps.css: #el-prompt code { background:#dbeafe; padding:1px 5px; border-radius:3px; font-family:monospace; font-size:0.9em; }

quizQuestions.q:
What does this print?

total = 0
for day in range(1, 8):
    sold = int(input())
    total = total + sold
print(total)

Inputs: 10 10 10 10 10 10 10

What is printed?

price = float(input())
print("Total:", price * 3)

Input: 4.50

What does this print?

for day in range(1, 4):
    prompt = "Day " + str(day) + ": "
    print(prompt)

The user types 3 then 7 then -1. What is total?

total = 0
n = int(input())
while n != -1:
    total = total + n
    n = int(input())
print(total)

The user types 60, 40, 80, 20, -1. How many passed (score >= 50)?

passes = 0
score = int(input())
while score >= 0:
    if score >= 50:
        passes = passes + 1
    score = int(input())
print(passes)

What is the bug?

age = int(input("Age: "))
while age < 11 and age > 18:
    print("Invalid")
    age = int(input("Age: "))

Inputs are 5.0, 5.0, 5.0. What does this print?

total = 0.0
for i in range(1, 4):
    total = total + float(input())
print("Average:", total / 3)

The user types 200, 150, 200. What is printed?

total = 0.0
while total < 500:
    deposit = float(input())
    total = total + deposit
print("Done:", total)
What does colour.lower() == "red" evaluate to when colour = "RED"?

The user types 8 hours each day for 5 days. Hourly rate is 15. What is the wage?

total_hours = 0
for day in range(1, 6):
    hours = float(input())
    total_hours = total_hours + hours
wage = total_hours * 15
print(wage)

Inputs: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. What is printed?

total = 0
for i in range(1, 6):
    mark = int(input())
    total = total + mark
print("Average:", total / 5)
